summary:
- publisher=EIA, title=World Oil Transit Chokepoints (PDF), directness=high, why=Comprehensive 2024 update on global chokepoints including Strait of Hormuz with tanker tracking data and historical context, next=https://www.eia.gov/international/content/analysis/special_topics/World_Oil_Transit_Chokepoints/wotc.pdf  
- publisher=EIA, title=“The Strait of Hormuz is the world's most important oil transit chokepoint” (Today in Energy), directness=high, why=Explicitly identifies Hormuz as top chokepoint using Vortexa tanker data; confirms EIA’s authoritative monitoring role, next=https://www.eia.gov/Todayinenergy/detail.php?id=61002  
- publisher=EIA, title=Strait of Hormuz Special Topic Page, directness=medium, why=Dedicated portal for Hormuz analysis; likely contains updated charts, timelines, and disruption history not in PDF or Today in Energy, next=https://www.eia.gov/international/analysis/special-topics/Strait_of_Hormuz.php  
- publisher=EIA, title=World Oil Transit Chokepoints (web version), directness=medium, why=Updated to March 2026; integrates Short-Term Energy Outlook and Vortexa data — likely supersedes 2024 PDF with latest transit volumes and disruption modeling, next=https://www.eia.gov/international/content/analysis/special_topics/World_Oil_Transit_Chokepoints  
- publisher=EIA, title=EIA Press Release: Hormuz closure as key forecast driver (04/07/2026), directness=high, why=Confirms Hormuz closure scenarios are actively modeled in EIA’s latest forecasts; implies recent disruption events or geopolitical risks are influencing 2026 projections, next=https://www.eia.gov/pressroom/releases/press586.php
